title: Posthuman, All Too Human: Towards a New Process Ontology
author: Braidotti

opening_move.description: The paper opens by positioning Haraway's work as Continental philosophy in the French tradition of bodily materialism and as a pioneering example of 'high post-humanism', proposing to read it through the lens of nomadic subjectivity and process ontology.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Reading Haraway alongside Deleuze enables a non-oedipal, affirmative posthuman ontology that goes beyond linguistic constructivism toward a materialist account of becoming.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper proposes a synthesis between Haraway's feminist posthumanism and Deleuze's process ontology, positioning both against psychoanalytic essentialism and linguistic paradigms, to develop a materialist, affirmative theory of the nomadic posthuman subject.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical close reading and synthesis: reading Haraway through Deleuze, Continental phenomenology, and Foucaultian biopolitics to construct an alternative posthumanist ontology.
epistemic_mode.notes: The argument is entirely within philosophical-theoretical space. Haraway's figurations (cyborg, oncomouse, companion species) are interpreted as philosophical figures, not empirical objects.

case_type.case_name: Haraway's posthumanist figurations (cyborg, oncomouse, companion species)

confidence_notes: This is a theoretical essay engaging with Haraway's work through Braidotti's own nomadic philosophy. The piece is primarily a philosophical synthesis rather than an analysis of Haraway's texts per se; it uses Haraway to develop Braidotti's own position. Move sequence is unusual: case interpretation (reading Haraway) is central and takes most of the paper, with the framework proposal emerging through the interpretation.
